chapters.name: The Best Christmas Present in the World

subtopics.name: HoneyDew 8- The Best Christmas Present in the World

questions.question: What did the author find in the small black tin box? Answer in the context of The Best Christmas Present in the World.
What did Hans Wolf do as a civilian?
questions.explanation: As a civilian, Hans Wolf played Cello in the orchestra.

questions.question: Who waved the white flag from the trenches?

options.option: One of the English soldiers generally referred to as Tommy
One of the German soldiers generally referred to as Fritz.

questions.explanation: The general term for German soldiers to be referred to as Fritz.

questions.question: “Ah, Dorset,” he smiled. “I know this place. I know it very well.”  How did Hans know about Dorset?

options.option: Hans Wolf knew about Dorset from reading books in English

questions.explanation: Thomas Hardy was the favourite writer of Hans Wolf. He read the novel Far from the Madding Crowd. The story was set in Dorset.

questions.question: What is Marzipan?

options.option: A sweet covering on a cake made from sugar, eggs, and almonds
A sweet yellow or white paste of ground almonds, sugar, and egg whites, used to coat pizzas

questions.explanation: Generally, a cake is coated with marzipan. Jim shared his Christmas cake with Hans and he felt that marzipan was the best he had ever tasted.

questions.question: Dearest Connie, by Christmas time next year, this war will be nothing but a distant and terrible memory. What did Jim mean by this sentence?

options.option: The war is just a distant memory
The war has already become a memory

questions.explanation: The war will end but it will leave a terrible memory in the minds of the soldiers.

questions.question: What did the author of The Best Christmas Present in the World find in a junk shop?

options.option: The author found a very old box in a junk shop.
The author found a table in a junk shop.
The author found a very old 19th-century roll-top desk in a junk shop.
The author found a very old Gold coin in a junk shop.

questions.explanation: The author found a very old 19th-century roll-top desk in a junk shop. It was made of oak. It was in very bad condition. The roll-top was broken into several pieces. One of the legs was clumsily mended and there were scorch marks down one side.

questions.question: What did the author of The Best Christmas Present in the World find in a secret drawer?

options.option: In a secret drawer of a roll-top desk, the author found a tie pin.
In a secret drawer of a roll-top desk, the author found a letter.
In a secret drawer of a roll-top desk, the author found a small tin box.
In a secret drawer of a roll-top desk, the author found secret map.

questions.explanation: In a secret drawer of a roll-top desk, the author found a small tin box. There was a piece of lined note paper sello-taped to its top. It had, ‘Jim’s last letter, received January 25, 1915. To be buried with me when the time comes” written on it.

questions.question: Jim wrote the letter to tell his wife about a wonderful thing that had happened on Christmas day. What was the wonderful thing that had happened?

options.option: On Christmas day he got a wonderful gift.
On Christmas day, right in the middle of a war, the warring soldiers were making peace.
He wrote a letter to inform that he is coming home after the war is finished.

questions.explanation: Jim wrote the letter to tell his wife about a wonderful thing that had happened on Christmas day. The British and the Germans were engaged in a war, yet on this day, both the troops met in no man's land. It was a thing of wonder because right in the middle of a war, the warring soldiers were making peace.

questions.question: What jobs did Hans Wolf and Jim Macpherson have when they were not soldiers?

options.option: Hans Wolf played the cello in the orchestra, Jim Macpherson was a school teacher.
Hans Wolf was a teacher, Jim Macpherson was a judge.
Hans Wolf was Assistant journalist, Jim Macpherson was a photographer.
Hans Wolf was doing plays in the theater, Jim Macpherson was a teacher in school.

questions.explanation: Hans Wolf was from Dusseldorf. He played the cello in the orchestra. Jim Macpherson was a school teacher from Dorset.

questions.question: Who did Connie Macpherson think her visitor was?
questions.explanation: Connie Macpherson thought that her visitor was her husband, Jim.

questions.question: The narrator bought the desk from a junk shop because (The Best Christmas Present in the World)

options.option: the author wanted an ancient roll-top desk
such a desk was not available anywhere else
the narrator could not afford a new roll-top desk

questions.explanation: The narrator bought the desk from a junk shop because the narrator could not afford a new roll-top desk as they were far too expensive.

questions.question: According to Jim, a Christmas morning should be
questions.explanation: According to Jim, a Christmas morning should be cold and frosty.

questions.question: The Christmas celebrations were initiated by
questions.explanation: The Christmas celebrations were initiated by the Germans.

questions.question: Jim’s first reaction to the Happy Christmas by the Germans was one of
questions.explanation: Jim’s first reaction to the 'Happy Christmas' by the Germans was one of surprise.

questions.question: Schnapps is a (The Best Christmas Present in the World)
Sausage is a (The Best Christmas Present in the World)
questions.explanation: Sausage is a slice of meat and bread.

questions.question: What was the speaker’s reaction when he realizes that it was the other side that began it? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)
questions.explanation: The speaker says that I would like to tell that they began it. But was ashamed to say the truth that Fritz began it.

questions.question: On which date does Connie receive Jim’s letter?
questions.explanation: Connie received Jim's last letter on 25 January, 1915. The letter was dated December 26, 1914.

questions.question: What is the Best Christmas present in the world?
questions.explanation: Jim’s return was the best Christmas present in the world for Connie.

questions.question: By which Christmas did Jim promise to be back home?
questions.explanation: Jim had promised to come home by the Christmas of 1915.

questions.question: Who is the author of the lesson The Best Christmas Present in the World?
questions.explanation: Michael Morpurgo is the writer of the lesson.

questions.question: How was the condition of the roll-top desk?
questions.explanation: The roll-top desk was in a bad condition.

questions.question: What did the shallow space underneath the last drawer reveal? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)
questions.explanation: The shallow space underneath the last drawer revealed a secret drawer.

questions.question: What did the Germans offer to share with the Tommies?
Where did the two armies meet? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)
questions.explanation: The two armies met in no man's land.

questions.question: What did Hans Wolf do apart from being a soldier?
How did the German officer Hans Wolf know so much about Dorset?

options.option: Both from school and reading English books

questions.explanation: He had learned about England from school, and from reading books in English.

questions.question: What is that every soldier wants? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)

options.option: To be able to see their family again

questions.explanation: Every soldier wishes to see his family again soon, that the fighting would end and they could all go home.

questions.question: What happened to Connie’s house?
questions.explanation: Connie's house had caught fire but she was saved.

questions.question: Who did Connie confuse the author with?
questions.explanation: Connie mistook the author for Jim who had come home, as promised on Christmas.

questions.question: Who were Tommies? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)
Which of these most clearly conveys the importance of the letter? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)

options.option: It was locked in a tin box.
Connie wished to be buried with it.

questions.explanation: Connie wished to be buried with it.

questions.question:
But the truth, I'm ashamed to say, is that Fritz began it.
What does the statement show? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)

options.option: Jim regretted interacting with the Fritz officer.
Jim felt guilty about the whole incident.

questions.question: Who are Batsheba, Gabriel Oak and Sergeant Troy?
What does Jim mainly do in the letter?

options.option: Illustrate the struggles of soldiers during wartime
Explain how his army planned to put an end to the war
Describe his longing to be home for Christmas
Narrate an unlikely event of hope and compassion

questions.explanation: Narrate an unlikely event of hope and compassion

questions.question: Based on the events in the text, which statement is true? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)

options.option: Jim's optimism about returning home was misplaced.
Jim's army was being tricked by the Germans.
Jim did not believe he would survive the war.

questions.explanation: Jim's optimism about returning home was misplaced.

questions.question: What was the best Christmas present, according to Connie?

options.option: Sharing the marzipan cake with the visitor
The visitor reading the letter for her

questions.question: Which of these does the author do? (The Best Christmas Present in the World)

options.option: Narrates events from the past to set the context
Ends with a revelation that changes the plot
Starts the story with an exciting event
Teaches a life lesson through the characters

questions.explanation: Narrates events from the past to set the context

questions.question: Why did Mrs. Ignesious wake up early in the morning on the tsunami day?

options.option: she wanted to go for a walk

questions.explanation: She sensed an earthquake at 6 am that day.

questions.question: When did Tsunami hit sea coasts of India?
According to the text, The Tsunami, which phenomenon causes the occurrence of a tsunami?
questions.explanation: A tsunami is a very large and powerful wave caused by earthquakes under the sea.

questions.question: What is a tsunami, as described in the text The Tsunami?
questions.explanation: A tsunami is a very large and powerful wave that is caused due to earthquakes under the sea.

questions.question: In the text, The Tsunami, the stories in the first section are collected from which archipelago?
questions.explanation: The survival stories presented in the first section of the text are collected from the Andaman and Nicobar archipelago.

questions.question: Which word from the text The Tsunami means the same as a group of many islands and the surrounding sea?
questions.explanation: The archipelago is a word that describes a group of many islands and the surrounding sea, collectively. The group of islands in Andaman and Nicobar is referred to as the Andaman and Nicobar archipelago which suffered a terrible crisis in 2004 due to the tsunami that hit the place.

questions.question: In the text The Tsunami, how old were, Tilly and her sister respectively when they witnessed the tsunami?
questions.explanation: Tilly Smith was a ten-year-old girl and her sister was seven years old when they had witnessed the tsunami that hit Thailand in 2004.

questions.question:
In the text The Tsunami, what was Sanjeev's occupation?

Enable GingerCannot connect to Ginger Check your internet connection
or reload the browserDisable in this text fieldEditEdit in Ginger4Edit in Ginger×
questions.explanation: Sanjeev was a policeman, serving in the Katchall island of the Nicobar group of islands.

questions.question: In the text The Tsunami, how old was Meghna when she witnessed the disaster?
questions.explanation: Meghna was thirteen years old when she witnessed the disaster. She swept away along with her parents and seventy-seven other people.

questions.question: Where did Almas's family go to celebrate Christmas when the disaster occurred, in the text The Tsunami?
questions.explanation: Almas went to Nancowry island at her mother's home to celebrate Christmas when she witnessed the tsunami that hit Andaman and Nicobar on 26 December 2004. She lost her family in the disaster.

questions.question: Where did Almas find herself after she regained her consciousness, in the text The Tsunami?
questions.explanation: Almas's family was swept away by the waves of the tsunami though she survived by climbing on a floating log of wood. She had fainted soon after. She woke up in a hospital at Kamorta from where she was taken back to Port Blair.

questions.question: In the text The Tsunami, how did Tilly Smith realize that a tsunami was about to hit Thailand?
questions.explanation: Tilly and her family were at the beach when the sea started to swell up. She recollected a geography lesson that was taught at her school two weeks before she came to Thailand and remembered that she had been taught about tsunamis and earthquakes.

questions.question: Where did the Smith family belong from, as mentioned in the text The Tsunami?
questions.explanation: The Smith family belonged from South-East England and were celebrating Christmas at a beach resort in southern Thailand.

questions.question: According to the text The Tsunami, when did the tsunami hit Thailand?
questions.explanation: The tsunami hit Thailand and parts of India on 26 December 2004. It had been triggered by a massive earthquake off northern Sumatra on that day.

questions.question: In the Tsunami, where did the earthquake take place that caused the tsunami in Thailand and parts of India?
questions.explanation: The tsunami that hit Thailand and parts of India on 26 December 2004 was triggered by a massive earthquake in northern Sumatra early that morning.

questions.question: Who made the following statement in the text The Tsunami - The water was swelling and kept coming in?
questions.explanation: Penny Smith recollected her memories of Thailand during the tsunami when they were saved by their little daughter Tilly. She said that the sea was swelling up and the water kept coming in but they could not understand what was going on until Tilly became quite hysterical claiming of an earthquake under the sea.

questions.question: As discussed in the text The Tsunami, why did Ignesious's wife wake him up at six in the morning?
questions.explanation: Ignesious's wife woke him up in the morning around six because she felt an earthquake.

questions.question: In the text The Tsunami, where did Ignesious work as a co-operative society's manager?
questions.explanation: Ignesious was the manager of a co-operative society in Katchall.

questions.question: Why did the elephants run for higher ground before the tsunami hit the coasts of Sri Lanka, in The Tsunami?
questions.explanation: It is said that animals can understand nature better than men. It has been observed in Sri Lanka, that before the tsunami hit the Sri Lankan coasts, the elephants screamed and ran for higher ground to seek protection for themselves since they were aware of the disaster before took place.

questions.question: According to many people, what do animals possess, as discussed in the text The Tsunami?
questions.explanation: It has been observed that animals often sense the arrival of a disaster much before humans can detect it. There were eyewitnesses in Sri Lanka who observed that before the place suffered a great loss by the tsunami, animals took shelter to keep themselves protected. They seem to possess a sixth sense.

questions.question: According to the text The Tsunami, how many species of birds are found at the Yala National Park in Sri Lanka?
questions.explanation: The Yala National Park in Sri Lanka was home to a variety of animals, including elephants, leopards, and 130 species of birds.

questions.question: What is another word for shelter from the text The Tsunami?
questions.explanation: The animals that survived the tsunami took refuge before the tsunami hit the coastal regions. This explains that they found their shelter to keep themselves protected.

questions.question: Sanjeev has swept away

options.option: because he was a policeman on duty near the sea
because he was standing at the shore

questions.explanation: Sanjeev has swept away while trying to save someone else. (Sanjeev jumped into the water to rescue the wife of the guesthouse cook, but they were both swept away).

questions.question: The little girl who saved many lives during the tsunami rage was ________.
questions.explanation: The little girl who saved many lives during the tsunami rage was Tilly Smith.

questions.question: The tsunami waves were triggered by a massive earthquake:
questions.explanation: The tsunami waves were triggered by a massive earthquake in northern Sumatra.

questions.question: How did Tilly become aware of the upcoming calamity?
questions.explanation: Tilly remembered the video of a tsunami that her geography teacher had shown her class. The teacher had also told them that tsunamis can be caused by earthquakes, volcanoes, and landslides

questions.question: What was the occupation of Sanjeev? (The Tsunami)
questions.explanation: Sanjeev was a policeman, serving in the Katchall island of the Nicobar group of islands

questions.question: Where was Tilly’s school?
Where is the Yala National Park situated? (The Tsunami)
questions.explanation: The Yala National Park is situated in Sri Lanka.

questions.question: Why were no animal carcasses found? (The Tsunami)
questions.explanation: Perhaps animals possess a sixth sense. They can feel the tremor (approaching disaster) long before humans realise.  So not many animals were reported dead.

questions.question: How many times did Meghna see the relief helicopters?
questions.explanation: Eleven times she saw the relief helicopters fly overhead, but they did not see her.

questions.question: Who was Almas Javed? (The Tsunami)
questions.explanation: Almas Javed was ten years old. She was a student of Carmel Convent in Port Blair.

questions.question: Whom did Sanjeev try to save? (The Tsunami)
questions.explanation: Sanjeev tried to save the wife of the guesthouse cook.

questions.question: Who was John? (The Tsunami)
What did Meghna hold on to in the water?
questions.explanation: Meghna spent two days floating in the sea, holding on to a wooden door.

questions.question: How old was Almas? (The Tsunami)
questions.explanation: Almas Javed was ten years old. She was a student of Carmel Convent in Port Blair where her father had a petrol pump.

questions.question: Where did Almas live? (The Tsunami)
Which member of the Smith’s family recognised it was a tsunami?
questions.explanation: Tilly Smith recognized that it was a tsunami.

questions.question: Meghna saved herself from the tsunami by

options.option: the people who were swept away with her

questions.explanation: Meghna saved herself from a tsunami by holding on to a wooden door.

questions.question: Penny Smith's initial reaction to the tsunami was
What convinced Penny Smith to leave the beach? (The Tsunami)
What is the main idea of the third section? (The Tsunami)

options.option: Animals seem to be able to sense natural calamities early
Animals were notaffected much by the tsunami
Animals in different places react differently to natural calamities
Animals helped save many humans from the tsunami

questions.explanation: Animals seem to be able to sense natural calamities early

questions.question: The second paragraph of the third section begins with (The Tsunami)
In the third section, the writer mainly (The Tsunami)
How are the three sections of the text related to each other? (The Tsunami)

options.option: They analyse the cause of related incidents.
They place a series of incidents in proper sequence.
They relate the same incident from different perspectives.

questions.explanation: They relate the same incident from different perspectives.

questions.question: Which regulation was passed in 1818 to oppress the Indians?
questions.explanation: The Bengal State Prisoners Regulation, III of 1818, was a law for preventive detention enacted by the East India Company in the Presidency of Bengal in 1818.

questions.question: Who was Tipu Sultan?
questions.explanation: He was a brave ruler of Mysore.

questions.question: The text titled Glimpses of Past has been represented in which form?
questions.explanation: The text has been pictorially represented with speech bubbles that form the narrative of the prose.

questions.question: What is the context of the prose Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The text Glimpses of the Past states the conditions that led to India's First War of Independence through its pictorial representations and speech bubbles.

questions.question: Who is the creator of the text Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The prose Glimpses of the Past has been beautifully represented by the well-known Indian novelist S. D. Sawant.

questions.question: What were the reactions of the common men towards the British Conquests in the prose Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The common men knew that after the successive conquests of the British, they've become slaves to them.

questions.question:
In the prose Glimpses of the Past, who preached ideas of untouchability and child marriage?

Enable GingerCannot connect to Ginger Check your internet connection
or reload the browserDisable in this text fieldEditEdit in Ginger5Edit in Ginger×
questions.explanation: Religious leaders of India preached ideas of untouchability and child marriage. According to them, anyone who crosses the seas loses their religion.

questions.question: According to the religious leaders, who were the cause of all the misery in the world, discussed in Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The religious leaders preached of untouchability and child marriage in India. According to them, women were the cause of all the misery in the world.

questions.question: According to the prose Glimpses of the Past, how many Indians died of starvation between 1822 and 1836?
questions.explanation: Farmers were burdened with taxes by the British merchants and their crops were also taken away by them. As a result, famine struck and fifteen lakh Indians died of starvation between 1822 to 1836.

questions.question: Whom did the British policies ruin as discussed in the text Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The British policies ruined the expert artisans and their families on account of their own financial profits.

questions.question: Who was Raja Ram Mohan Roy, as mentioned in the text Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy was a learned man from Bengal who tried to bring about a revolutionary change and believed in science.

questions.question: According to Raja Ram Mohan Roy, what should knowledge be, as discussed in Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy believed that knowledge should be practical and scientific.

questions.question:
According to the text Glimpses of the Past, who had said that the essence of every religion is the same?

Enable GingerCannot connect to Ginger Check your internet connection
or reload the browserDisable in this text fieldEditEdit in Ginger4Edit in Ginger×
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy told his wife Uma that "there are cows of different colours but they give the milk of the same colour. Likewise, there are different religions but the essence of every religion is the same".

questions.question: Who were the oppressors as gathered from the text Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The Indians were oppressed by the British as rulers who came as merchants to the country.

questions.question: Who suggested a change in Indian education, according to the text  Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: Education in India was performed in two languages - Persian and Sanskrit. The British officers did not understand these languages, but an Englishman named Macaulay suggested a change in education in 1835.

questions.question: According to the prose Glimpses of the Past, education in India was carried out in which languages?
questions.explanation: Education in India was carried out in two languages - Persian and Sanskrit.

questions.question: What did the heavy taxes of the British force the farmers to do in the text Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The British being merchant only thought about their profit and their heavy taxes on the farmers led them to abandon their fields.

questions.question: According to the prose Glimpses of the Past, what attracted Raja Ram Mohan Roy?
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy was attracted by science and modern knowledge. He believed that knowledge should be practical and scientific.

questions.question: According to the prose Glimpses of the Past, who started newspapers?
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy had started newspapers, but the suspicious British stopped them in 1823.

questions.question: Who made the following statement in the prose Glimpses of the Past - The bones of cotton weavers are bleaching the plains of India?
questions.explanation: Governor-General Bentinck reported back home that the bones of the cotton weavers were bleaching the plains of India.

questions.question: What was sent from one village to another to tell men about their required service for their emperor in the prose Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: Chapaties were sent from village to village to tell the people that their emperor would want their service to fight against the British.

questions.question: What was circulated among the Indian soldiers as a message to be united against the British as discussed in the text Glimpses of the Past?
questions.explanation: The way chapaties were passed from village to village to build people's integrity, soldiers too circulated lotus flowers among themselves to unite against the British and fight for their independence.

questions.question: What were the social practices that were being preached by the religious leader? (Glimpses of the Past)
questions.explanation: Religious leaders preached ideas like untouchability, child marriage, and inferiority towards women.

questions.question: Who tried to end superstitions in India? (Glimpses of the Past)
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy tried to end superstitions in India.

questions.question: What was Raja Ram Mohan Roy fascinated by?
What was the situation of Indian cotton weavers?
questions.explanation: the situation of Indian cotton weavers was miserable.

questions.question: Which language did the Britishers want to change the education to?
questions.explanation: Education in India was in Persian and Sanskrit. In 1835, an Englishman named Macaulay suggested teaching the English language to the natives.

questions.question: What was the source of discontent that was brewing in the East India Company’s army?
questions.explanation: Discontent was brewing in the East India Company’s army too. The Indian soldiers noticed that the white soldier got huge pay, mansions to live in while they lived in servants' quarters. They got a pittance and slow promotions.

questions.question: Who was told that the grease on the bullet they have to bite is made from the fat of cows and pigs? (Glimpses of the Past)
questions.explanation: Brahmin soldiers were told that the grease on the bullet they have to bite is made from the fat of cows and pigs.

questions.question: Where did the sepoys march to?
What were people losing due to the new land rules? (Glimpses of the Past)
Who was the ruler of Lucknow who was bitter towards the British? (Glimpses of the Past)
questions.explanation: Many former rulers like Begum Hazrat Mahal of Lucknow were bitter towards the British as the Whites took away their kingdom.

questions.question: Whose name did Azimulla Khan suggest to be their leader in the War of Independence? (Glimpses of the Past)
questions.explanation: Azimulla Khan told Tatya Tope that Peshwa Nana Saheb should be their leader in this war of independence.

questions.question: Who was hit by a bullet in the wrist? (Glimpses of the Past)
questions.explanation: Eighty-year old Kunwar Singh of Bihar received a bullet in his wrist.

questions.question: Where did the patriots fight pitch battles? (Glimpses of the Past)
questions.explanation: The patriots pounced upon the British and fought pitched battles all over North India.

questions.question: Where did the leader Maulvi Ahmedulla belong to?
questions.explanation: Popular leaders like Maulvi Ahmedulla belonged to Faizabad.

questions.question: In the year 1765, in India: (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: East India Company ruled most parts of India
Indian princes ruled most of the country
the British Govt, ruled most of the country

questions.explanation: In the year 1765, in India Indian princes ruled most of the country.

questions.question: From 1757 to 1849, there was no peace in India because (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: The Indian Princes kept fighting each other

questions.explanation: From 1757 to 1849, there was no peace in India because the Indian Princes kept fighting each other constantly.

questions.question: The business community welcomed the British because they

options.option: British rule had brought a degree of peace

questions.explanation: The business community welcomed the British because British rule had brought a degree of peace.

questions.question: The worst sufferers under British rule were
questions.explanation: The worst sufferers under British rule were the poor farmers and artisans.

questions.question: In the early nineteenth century, Raja Ram Mohan Roy was
questions.explanation: In the early nineteenth century, Raja Ram Mohan Roy was an important reformer.

questions.question: Under Regulation III of the British rulers

options.option: Indians could be jailed without trial in a court

questions.explanation: Under Regulation III, the Indians could be jailed without trial in a court.

questions.question: What did the British impose on the Indian farmers?
questions.explanation: Being merchants, the British wanted quick profits, their heavy taxes forced farmers to abandon their fields.

questions.question: What was the value of the British goods exported to India in 1829?
questions.explanation: By 1829, Britain was exporting British goods worth seven crore rupees to India.

questions.question: Which Englishman reported the plight of India to England?
questions.explanation: Governor-General Bentinck reported the plight of India to England.

questions.question: What were the reasons behind the prospering British?
questions.explanation: The British prospered on the Company’s loot while Indian industries began to die.

questions.question: How were the Indian princes short-sighted? (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: They overlooked the superiority of British weapons.
They did notrespect far-sighted rulers like Tipu.
They gave the British opportunities to gain power.

questions.explanation: They gave the British opportunities to gain power.

questions.question: What does the panel How did Indians react to these conquests? convey? (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: It contrasts the attitudes of the princes and common men.
It shows that Indians were unhappy with the British rulers.

questions.explanation: It contrasts the attitudes of the princes and common men.

questions.question: According to Ram Mohan Roy, what was the solution to India's problems? (Glimpses of the Past)
Governor-General Bentick's words show that the British were ________. (Glimpses of the Past)
What is the main idea of the section Sparks? (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: The ways in which the British tortured the Indians
The response of the British to the revolt
The impacts of the rise in taxes

questions.question: What led the Indian soldiers to revolt? (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: Gopal had a habit of indulging in practical jokes.

questions.question: What do the sections Revolt and The Fight for Freedom together mainly convey? (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: Demonstrate the damage caused by the revolt
Show the impact of the revolt on British rule in India

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the text? (Glimpses of the Past)

options.option: To narrate the events that caused the revolt
To give information about the impacts of the revolt
To persuade the readers to honour freedom fighters
To describe the role of various sections of society in the revolt

questions.explanation: To narrate the events that caused the revolt

subtopics.name: HoneyDew 8- Bepin Choudhurys Lapse of Memory

questions.question: What was the purpose of Chunilal's visit to Bepin Babu’s residence?

options.option: To seek help to find a job

questions.explanation: Chunilal had visited Bepin Babu To seek help to find a job.

questions.question: What would Bepin Choudhury do every Monday?
questions.explanation: Every Monday Bepin Choudhury would go to Kalicharan's shop to buy books.

questions.question: In the prose Bepin Choudhury's Lapse of Memory, on which day every week did Bepin visit the book shop?
questions.explanation: Every Monday, while returning from work, Bepin Choudhury went to Kalicharan's in New Market to buy books.

questions.question: Why was Bepin disturbed after getting assured from everyone that he had been to Ranchi, in the text Bepin Choudhury's Lapse of Memory?
questions.explanation: Bepin Babu had asked almost everyone to confirm his whereabouts in October 1958 and got assured that he had been to Ranchi. But he himself couldn't recollect this particular incident of his life. He was so disturbed that he feared that he was losing his memory.

questions.question: In the prose Bepin Choudhury's Lapse of Memory, who played a trick on Bepin with his imagination?

questions.question: Who made the following statement - Boy, you've got me hemmed in in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?
questions.explanation: Penny was Jody's father. He was lying on the bed exhausted with his near escape from death when Jody came to him and demanded that he wanted to raise the fawn. Penny thought it would be unfair to leave the fawn alone in the forest, but he was worried if it would be able to live here. Nevertheless, he didn't want to say and thus said that he has got him hemmed in.

questions.question: Who taught Jody to differentiate between a male fawn and a female fawn in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?
questions.explanation: Jody's father had taught him to differentiate between a fawn and a doe-fawn. Pa had said that on a fawn, the spots were all in a line. On the contrary, in a doe-fawn, the spots were everywhere.

questions.question: Whom was Jody worried about in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?
questions.explanation: Jody was worried about the fawn that was left alone in the forest. It had been deprived of its mother by them and so he wanted to raise it.

Because Jody and Penny killed its mother

questions.explanation: Jody and Penny had to kill the doe which was the fawn's mother to save Penny's life. Penny was bit by a snake, so they had to kill the doe and used its liver to draw the poison out.

questions.question: Who made the following statement Nothing in the world comes quite free in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?
questions.explanation: Jody's mother was surprised to hear his wish to bring the fawn at their place and raise it to which Doctor Wilson said that he was quite right in wishing that. They had killed its mother to save Penny and deprived the fawn of its mother. So they should adopt the fawn because nothing in the world comes quite free.

questions.question: What did Jody feed the fawn in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?
questions.explanation: Jody had stored some milk for the fawn before he left to find it. He fed the fawn the milk that had been stored because he did not have anything else to feed the fawn.

questions.question: Which phrase from the text This is Jody's Fawn means the same as to act as a compass?
questions.explanation: Makes a bearing is a phrase that has been often used to denote an object which acted as a compass that helps people identify directions if they do not carry an actual compass with them. In the prose, it was the tall pine tree that made a bearing for Jody if he wanted to find his way back home.

questions.question: Who helped Jody reach the forest to find the fawn in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?
questions.explanation: Jody rode with Mill on his horseback to reach the forest and search for the fawn that he had seen there the previous day.

questions.question: Why did Jody carry the fawn with him in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?

options.option: Because the fawn was unable to walk
Because he wanted it to trust him
Because the fawn wanted to be carried

questions.explanation: Jody knew that the fawn was scared and could run away if it wanted to. He didn't want the fawn to be left alone in the forest as an abandoned child who doesn't know how to feed itself. He wanted to raise the fawn and keep it safe. He carried the fawn to assure its safety in his arms and help it trust him.

questions.question: Who saved Penny in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?
questions.explanation: Penny believed that the doe saved his life because they used the doe's liver to draw out the poison from his body as he was bitten by a snake.

questions.question: Why did Jody want to search for the fawn alone in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?

options.option: He didn't want to share the moment with him
He didn't want Mill to know the truth
He intended to go elsewhere with the fawn
He thought that the fawn may get scared

questions.explanation: Jody was all of a sudden unwilling to let Mill accompany him. He wanted the moment to be his own in every way. If the fawn was found to be dead, he didn't want his disappointment to be seen. On the contrary, if the fawn was alive, he wanted the moment to be a blissful secret experience between him and the fawn.

questions.question: What surrounded the carcass of the doe in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?

questions.question: According to Penny, how would a fawn follow somebody, as mentioned in the prose This is Jody's Fawn?

Relievingly, the fawn did follow him after it has been carried for the second time by Jody.

questions.explanation: The poet clearly comprehended that cities were developed near a source of water or flowing rivers as the waters supported the ecosystems and assisted the survival of all living beings.

questions.question: When did it become clear that the earth was round? (Geography Lesson)

options.option: At a height of ten thousand miles
At a height of six thousand miles
At a height of six inches to the mile

questions.question: What became clear when the jet reached ten thousand feet? (Geography lesson)
questions.explanation: When the jet reached ten thousand feet, it was clear why cities were situated near the rivers and why the valleys were populated and the logic of geography.

questions.question: There seemed an inevitability about what on the ground had looked haphazard. What do you mean by the word haphazard? (Geography Lesson)

questions.question: There seemed an inevitability about what on ground had looked haphazard. What do you mean by the word inevitability? (Geography Lesson)

The poem ends on a note of (Geography Lesson)

options.option: Geography is a primary factor in the course of human development.
Geography limits the potential of human development.

questions.question: In the poem Geography Lesson, the poet mainly
Who composed the poem The last bargain?
questions.explanation: The poem was composed by Rabindranath Tagore. The poet highlighted the importance of freedom by projecting a man who was seeking work but was adamant to keep his independence.

questions.question:
"Come and hire me", I cried, while in the morning I was
walking on the stone-paved road.
Who is the speaker? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The speaker in the given lines was a man who was looking for work. He wanted the job but was adamant about keeping his freedom and was not ready to forgo his independence in lieu of power or money.

questions.question:
I will hire you with my power.
Who is the speaker? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The king offered to hire the man who was looking for a job in the poem. The man while wandering on a stone-paved road came across the king who was coming in his chariot. He offered to hire the man's services with his power.

questions.question: What was the speaker of the poem, The last bargain asking for?
questions.explanation: The speaker of the poem, The last bargain was a man who was looking for a job. He encountered many people who tried to lure him but he was not ready to compromise his freedom in the bargain.

questions.question: Who tried to lure the man who was looking for a job with his bag of gold? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: An old man came across the man who was looking for a job and tried to hire him by luring him with his wealth.

questions.question: When was the speaker walking on the stone-paved road in search of a job? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The speaker was walking on the stone-paved road in search of a job and came across the king who tried to hire him with his power and influence.

questions.question: Where did the man come across the beautiful maid? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The man came across a beautiful maid standing in a garden that was full of beautiful flowers. The lady offered to hire the man with her smile and beauty.

questions.question: When did the man come across the beautiful maid who tried to hire him with her beauty? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The poet met the beautiful woman in a beautiful garden when the evening had set in. The lady offered to hire the man and pay him off with her charm and beauty.

questions.question: Whom did the man meet till the evening in the poem, The last bargain?
questions.explanation: The man encountered the king in the morning. Towards mid of the day, he met the old man, and in the evening, he came across the fair maid.

questions.question: Where did the speaker of the poem, The last bargain meet the child ?
questions.explanation: The man while wandering at the seashore in search of a job came across a child who was playing with shells.

questions.question: Who captured the man's attention while wandering on the seashore? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The man reached the seashore when the sun was setting down and it shone brightly on the sand where an innocent child was engrossed playing with shells.

questions.question: What was the child doing when the speaker of the poem, The last bargain spotted him?
questions.explanation: The child was busy playing with shells when the man came across him at the seashore.

questions.question: What was the child's attitude towards the speaker when he first met him at the seashore? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The child looked up at the man, who had been wandering throughout the day in search of a job. It seemed as if the child understood the man completely. He offered to hire his services but didn't offer to pay anything in return.

questions.question: Whose offer did the man turn down in the poem, The last bargain?
questions.explanation: All three of them tried to hire the man's services but failed to do so. The king offered to buy his services with power, the old man offered to pay with his gold and the beautiful lady tried to lure him with her beauty.

questions.question: Whose offer did the man accept in the poem, The last bargain?
questions.explanation: The man wandered throughout the day in search of a job and finally accepted the child's offer. The child offered to hire the services of the man but didn't offer to pay anything in return. The man willingly accepted this offer for in playing with the child he continued to be free.

questions.question: How did the man feel after accepting the child's offer? (The last bargain)
questions.explanation: The man felt happy and relieved after accepting the child's offer for he was not required to compromise his freedom, as in the return of his services he was to get goodwill and happiness. This was perhaps the most desirable bargain that the man had been looking for.

questions.question: Why does the man accept the child’s offer?
questions.explanation: The man finalised the child's deal because it made him feel like a free man.

questions.question: What did the young maiden offer to hire him with?
questions.explanation: A young lovely maiden came out and offered to hire him with her smile.

questions.question: The fair maid wanted the poet (The Last Bargain)
questions.explanation: The fair maid wanted the poet to praise her smiles.

questions.question: What does the poet want at the beginning of the poem? (The Last Bargain)

options.option: He wants someone to join him on his journey.
He wants someone to listen to his sorrows.

questions.question: Which word best describes the poet's attitude towards the King's power? (The Last Bargain)

Her smile paled and melted into tears
What does the poet try to indicate in the line above? (The Last Bargain)

options.option: The maid’s sadness at the poet’s rejection

questions.explanation: The maid’s sadness at the poet’s rejection

questions.question: How does the tone in the third stanza change? (The Last Bargain)
What made the poet accept the child's offer? (The Last Bargain)

options.option: He felt that it would give him liberty.
He did not want to hurt the child.
He felt the child's innocence was trustworthy.

questions.explanation: He felt that it would give him liberty.

questions.question: Why did the poet say that the bargain had made him a free man?

options.option: He could go where he wanted to.
It ensured that he was not in anyone's debt.
It allowed him to look after the child as he wanted.

questions.explanation: It ensured that he was not in anyone's debt.

questions.question: What main idea is conveyed in the poem? (The Last Bargain)

options.option: Pride in wealth, money and beauty will lead to downfall.
It is important to balance material needs with spirituality.
Freedom of the spirit is more important than worldly comforts.
Children possess the inner wisdom that adults lack.

questions.explanation: Freedom of the spirit is more important than worldly comforts.

questions.question: In the poem The School Boy, when does the poet loved to rise?
questions.explanation: The poet in the poem said that he loved to rise in a summer morning when the birds sang on every tree.

questions.question: Who is described as a sweet company by the poet in the poem The School Boy?
questions.explanation: The poet referred to the skylark as a sweet companion because it tuned with him.

questions.question: Who sings with the schoolboy in the poem The School Boy?
questions.explanation: According to the poet, the skylark sang with the schoolboy, giving him a sweet company.

questions.question: According to the child in the poem The School Boy, what is a bird born for?
questions.explanation: The poet in the guise of a child in the poem believes that a bird is born for joy, and wondered how it can sing in a cage. This statement in the poem explicitly shows the harsh reality of the world where birds are caged for entertaining us with their song.

questions.question: Who dictates the little children in the school, as mentioned in the poem The School Boy?
questions.explanation: The poet expressed how gloomy the children were at school being dictated under a cruel eye. The cruel eye is a metaphor for a strict teacher who doesn't treat the children with mercy.

questions.question: What drives all the joy away? (The School Boy)

questions.question: The word winds mean (The School Boy)
questions.explanation: The distant huntsman winds his horn means makes a sound by blowing the horn. It expresses the mood of a summer morning in the countryside and is full of nature and countryside imagery.

questions.question: What does the boy like about the huntsman? (The School Boy)
questions.explanation: In the first stanza, the poet says that the boy likes the sound of the distance huntsman when he blows his horn. He enjoys the company of a small brown bird that sings with him.

questions.question: What does the poet mainly talk about in the first stanza? (The School Boy)

options.option: The desire to befriend the birds in his garden
The things that he loves about morning
The sights on his way to school
The things that makes staying at home enjoyable

options.option: The poet feels that teachers don't allow students freedom.
The poet is tired after studying hard.
The poet tries hard to stay awake during lessons.
The poet spends all day in school crying.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the third stanza? (The School Boy)

options.option: Uses an interesting sound to convey pain
Uses sarcasm to add drama to the situation

options.option: The beauty of a caged bird’s song
The loss of children’s freedom in schools
How fear suppresses the talent of students

options.option: To describe the ways in which children try to escape school

subtopics.name: HoneyDew 8- On the Grasshopper and Cricket

questions.question: Who composed the poem, On the grasshopper and cricket?
questions.explanation: The poem is a sonnet written by John Keats. It was inspired by the eternal beauty of nature.

questions.question: In which season did the grasshopper provide music? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: The grasshoppers produce music in summers so as to keep the music of earth alive since the birds curb their chirping during summers.

questions.question: In which season did the birds hide and remain silent in the poem, On the grasshopper and cricket?
questions.explanation: The birds felt uncomfortable and appear to faint due to excessive heat in the summer season so, they hid under a shady tree and remained quiet.

questions.question: Where do birds take rest on a hot summer day in the poem, On the grasshopper and cricket?
questions.explanation: The birds take rest under the shady trees to protect themselves from the scorching heat of the sun during summers.

questions.question: Who took rest beneath some pleasant weeds in the poem, On the grasshopper and cricket?
questions.explanation: The grasshopper took rest beneath some pleasant weeds after singing tirelessly and rejuvenating the human soul with its song.

questions.question: Whose voice ran from hedge to hedge in the poem, On the grasshopper and cricket?
questions.explanation: The poet highlights that the grasshopper sings tirelessly and bestows a soothing effect on mankind during summers.

questions.question: Who fainted due to heat during summer? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: The birds became quiet and appeared to faint due to the excessive heat from the blazing sun during the summer months.

questions.question: What effect did the grasshopper's song have on the human mind? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: The grasshopper's song created a comforting effect on the human minds and provided them with relief from the scorching heat of the sun during summers.

questions.question: What does the phrase, the poetry of earth refer to?
questions.explanation: The poet craftily focused on the beautiful sounds that are found on earth and have the power to soothe the human mind.

questions.question: Whose voice was heard from the stones? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: The voice of cricket seemed to spread an ever-increasing warmth through its songs during winters.

questions.question: Who was lost in drowsiness in the poem, On the grasshopper and cricket​​​​​?
questions.explanation: The poet projected the people to be sleepy and lost in drowsiness owing to the harsh winter conditions.

questions.question: What did the cricket's song spread? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: The cricket's song had a comforting effect on the human mind and it provided warmth and shielded them from the bitterness of frost in winters.

questions.question: What did the birds stop doing in the summers? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: Due to the extremely warm and uncomfortable weather, the birds ceased to sing and appeared to be silent.

questions.question: What gives a soothing effect on the human mind? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: Music has a soothing effect on the human mind. Nature has immense power to bestow us with peace and comfort. All these sounds reflect the eternal poetry of earth, which has the power to rejuvenate and heal the human soul with its serenity.

questions.question: What cast silence on the surroundings during winters? (On the grasshopper and cricket)
questions.explanation: Elements of winter like the frost are spread all over during the winters. The poet projected the frost to cast a blanket of silence on the surroundings.

questions.question:

questions.explanation: The frost is personified as a person who brings or casts a silence all over.

questions.question: Whose warmth is ever increasing? (On the Grasshopper and Cricket)
questions.explanation: The cricket’s song has a warmth which is ever increasing.

questions.question: Who sings the poetry of earth in the summer season?
questions.explanation: Grasshopper takes the lead during the summer when all the birds hide and protect. The grasshopper sings the poetry of the earth during summers.

questions.question: Where does the grasshopper take rest?
questions.explanation: He rests at ease beneath some pleasant weed.

questions.question: What has the frost wrought? (On the Grasshopper and Cricket)
questions.explanation: In lonely winter evenings, when frost brings the silence.

questions.question: Who sings the poetry of earth in the winter season?
questions.explanation: In lonely winter evenings, the sound of the cricket’s song brings warmth.

questions.question: What makes the birds faint? (On the Grasshopper and Cricket)
questions.explanation: When all the birds are faint with the hot sun.

questions.question: The winter evening is lone because (On the Grasshopper and Cricket)
questions.explanation: The winter evening is lone because there is silence all around.

questions.question:

options.option: There is always something to do on earth.

According to the poet, the grasshopper finds the summer

options.option: They make noise in the same season.
They make noise when other animals are quiet.

options.option: Suggest that the earth is never silent
Convince people to listen to the sounds of nature

questions.explanation: Suggest that the earth is never silent

subtopics.name: It So Happened 8 - How the Camel got his hump

questions.question: The animals which complained about the camel to the Djinn were:
questions.explanation: Dog, horse and ox complained about the camel to the Djinn

questions.question: There was one lazy animal that did nothing, and said nothing but ‘Humph’ Which one of the following was that?
Who asked the Camel to plough?
How many days can the Camel work without eating?
What did the Camel say when anybody spoke to him?
“...he has never yet learnt to behave”. In the light of this, what is the writer’s opinion about the camel?

options.option: The camel is still a lazy animal.

questions.explanation: According to the writer, the camel is still a very lazy animal that is unwilling to work. After he got the hump, the Djinn asked him to go out of the desert to help the other three animals. It seems that the camel still did not behave himself and worked lazily in the desert itself. It is for this reason that he was still unable to complete the pending work of those three missed days; and he continued carrying the hump and living in the desert.

questions.question: What, according to the Djinn, was the use of the “humph”?

options.option: It made the camel look better than the others.
The camel could work for three days without eating.

questions.explanation: According to the Djinn, the hump was useful for storing his food and using it during continuous work for many days.

questions.question: The camel was looking at this own reflection in the pool. What does it suggest to you about the camel?

options.option: It suggests that the camel was getting ready to for a party.
It suggests that the camel was proud of his looks.
It suggests that the camel loved wasting time.
It suggests that the camel disliked himself.

questions.explanation: The camel's looking at his own reflection in the pool shows that he was proud of his appearance, especially his back. He liked admiring himself.

questions.question: How did the Djinn know the horse was complaining against the camel?

options.option: he said that it had long legs and a long neck.
the camel had already told him about the fight they had had.
he always got complaints about the camel from others.

questions.explanation: The Djinn knew that the horse was complaining against the camel because he was the in charge of all deserts and knew about everything happening in relation to his deserts.

questions.question: What made the dog, the horse and the ox very angry?

options.option: the camel didn't work so they had to work double-time.
they disliked one another's company and were asked to work together.
they were asked to leave the desert.

questions.explanation: The man told the dog, the horse and the ox that since the camel cannot work, he would leave it alone and the three of them would have to work double-time to make up for the work not done by the camel. This made the three of them very angry.

questions.question: Why did the camel live in the middle of the desert?

options.option: The camel lived in the desert because his parents lived there.
The camel lived in the desert because he loved the place.
The camel lived in the desert because he worked there.
The camel lived in the desert because he did not want to work.

questions.explanation: The camel lived in the middle of the desert because it did not want to work. It ate sticks, thorns, and prickles, and when anybody spoke to it, it said “Humph!” and nothing more.

questions.question: What tasks, do you think, were assigned to the dog and the ox?

options.option: The dog and the ox were asked to trot and to play.
The dog was asked to fetch things and the ox to plough fields.
The dog and the ox were asked to take rest.

questions.explanation: The dog was assigned the task of fetching and carrying sticks, while the ox was assigned the task of ploughing.

questions.question: How was the camel portrayed in the plot, How the camel got his hump?
questions.explanation: The camel was projected to be extremely lazy and unwilling to work in the story. He was irresponsible and did not care about other animals.

questions.question: Which word did the camel utter repeatedly in the plot, How the camel got his hump?
questions.explanation: The camel uttered the word humph whenever anybody spoke to it or tried to approach it.

questions.question: Where did the camel live? (How the camel got his hump)
questions.explanation: The camel resided in the middle of a howling desert as he was absolutely reluctant to work.

questions.question: What did the camel eat ? (How the camel got his hump)
questions.explanation: The camel ate sticks, thorns, and prickles that he found in the middle of the howling desert where he lived.

questions.question: When did the horse approach the camel to ask him to work?
questions.explanation: The horse came to the camel on Monday morning to convince him to work along with it.

questions.question: Who approached the camel after the horse in the plot, How the camel got his hump?
questions.explanation: The dog came to the camel after the horse left after convincing him to fetch and carry like the other dogs.

questions.question: Which animal approached the camel with a stick in its mouth?
questions.explanation: The dog came to the camel, with a stick in its mouth to ask him to work along.

questions.question: Which animal was assigned the task to plough in the plot, How the camel got his hump?
questions.explanation: The ox came to the camel and asked the camel to do ploughing with it.

questions.question: ________ came to the camel, with the yoke on his neck.
questions.explanation: The ox approached the camel, with a yoke on its neck. The ox was probably wearing a string with bells on its neck.

questions.question: What did the man do on hearing about the camel's denial to work?

options.option: He asked the other three animals to compensate

questions.explanation: The man got enraged on hearing the camel's refusal to work and asked the other three animals to work more to compensate for the damage. He told them to work double-time to make up for the work, not by the camel.

questions.question: To whom did the other three animals complain about the camel? (How the camel got his hump)
questions.explanation: The ox, horse, and dog complained to the Djinn about the camel's irresponsible behaviour as he was in charge of all the deserts.

questions.question: How did the horse, dog, and the ox react to hearing the man's punishment?
questions.explanation: The three animals were full of rage on hearing that they had to work double-shift to make up for the loss of work not done by the camel.

questions.question: What work did the domesticated animals do in the plot, How the camel got his hump?
questions.explanation: The domesticated animals rendered the services of fetching and carrying loads, plowing, and drawing carts.

questions.question: Who was in charge of all the deserts in the plot, How the camel got his hump​​​​​​?
questions.explanation: The Djinn, a spirit that possessed strange powers was projected to be in charge of all the deserts in the story.

questions.question: Which animal wanted to stay idle in the plot, How the camel got his hump?
questions.explanation: The camel was potrayed as lazy and absolutely reluctant to work in the story.

questions.question: On which day did the camel get a hump on its back?
questions.explanation: The Djinn punished the camel for being irresponsible and not working by puffing up his back into a big hump on Thursday.

questions.question: Where was the camel looking when the Djinn approached it?
questions.explanation: The camel was looking at his own reflection in the pool of water when the Djinn spotted him in the desert.

questions.question: How could the camel go on without eating for three days in the plot, How the camel got his hump?
questions.explanation: The camel's hump could store food and water and provide it with sufficient energy to work.

questions.question: Camel lived in the Howling Desert because

options.option: he didn’t want to work for a man

questions.explanation: Camel lived in the Howling Desert because he didn’t want to work for a man.

questions.question: The dog went to the Camel
questions.explanation: The Dog went to the Camel with a stick in his mouth.

questions.question: The Ox asked the Camel
questions.explanation: The Ox asked the Camel to plough the field.

questions.question: A man called the Horse, the Dog, and the Ox together and asked them

questions.question: What was the reaction of the camel on the panchayat?

options.option: The camel came to the panchayat and begged pardon
The camel threatened the members of the panchayat
The camel didn’t come to the panchayat
The camel laughed at the panchayat and said “Humph”

questions.question: The Djinn came to the Panchayat (How the Camel got his hump)
questions.explanation: The Djinn who was in charge of All Deserts, came to the panchayat rolling in a cloud of dust.

questions.question: Who complained against the camel to the Djinn?

questions.question: When the Djinn went to the Camel

the Camel asked him why he had come

questions.question: The camel said, Humph repeatedly. How did it affect him?

options.option: He did not have to work like others
He was asked to leave the desert

questions.explanation: His back, that he was so proud of, suddenly puffed up into a great big hump

questions.question: Whom did the animals start to work for? (How the Camel got his hump)
questions.explanation: In the beginning, when the world was new and the animals were just beginning to work for Man.

questions.question: What did the Camel not want to do?
What was the reply of the camel to everyone?
questions.explanation: When anybody spoke to the camel, he said “Humph!” Just “Humph!” and nothing more.

questions.question: Who came to the camel on the morning with a saddle on his back?
questions.explanation: The Horse came to him on Monday morning with a saddle on his back.

questions.question: On which day did the Horse come to the camel?
questions.explanation: The Horse came to him on Monday morning with a saddle on his back.

questions.question: Who said, "Camel, O Camel, come out and trot like the rest of us"?
questions.explanation: The Horse came to him and said, “Camel, O Camel, come out and trot like the rest of us.”

questions.question: Who first attempted to talk to the camel?

questions.question: Who attempted to talk to the camel secondly?
questions.explanation: The Dog attempted to talk to the camel secondly.

questions.question: Who did not want to work? (How the Camel got his hump)
questions.explanation: The Camel did not want to work.

questions.question: What did the camel do in the panchayat meeting?
What was the camel doing when Djinn found him?

options.option: He was resting under the shade of a tree
He was looking at his own reflection in the water

questions.question: How did the Djinn sit down?

questions.question: What bulged out on the camel's back?

questions.question: For how many days could a camel work with his hump without eating food?

questions.question: Name of the author of How the camel got his hump.

questions.question: Velu scratched his head and sighed. I’ll do it for now, he thought, until I find a better job. What was that?
