questions.question: How old was Velu in the plot, Children at work?
questions.explanation: Velu was an eleven-year-old boy when he ran away from his home in the story, Children at work.

questions.question: Where did Velu run away to?
questions.explanation: Velu boarded a train to Chennai without a ticket. He ran away from his home and wandered around for hours before getting on a train to Chennai.

questions.question: Which train did Velu board to Chennai?
What was Velu carrying in his bundle to Chennai?
questions.explanation: The poor boy ran away from his home and carried only a small bundle that contained a shirt, a comb, and a towel inside.

questions.question: What was the strange girl wearing when she met Velu for the first time at the railway platform?
questions.explanation: The girl was wearing a long banian that came down to her knees. She was very poor and did not possess appropriate clothing or accessories.

questions.question: Why did Velu run away from his home in the plot, Children at work?

options.option: He had a fight with his sisters

questions.explanation: Velu's father was a drunkard, he used to snatch away all the money that his children earned. Velu was tired of being thrashed by his father every day and the poor boy ran away from home when he couldn't tolerate the harassment anymore.

questions.question: How did Velu feel about reaching Chennai Central?
questions.explanation: Velu felt miserable and exhausted on reaching Chennai station. He sat on a bench feeling sad. He was hungry, tired, and slightly scared at the station. The poor child was homeless and had no money with him.

questions.question: How much fare did Velu pay to reach Chennai by train?
questions.explanation: Velu travelled to Chennai by train without a ticket. He had no money for the ticket and luckily the ticket collector did not come to the unreserved compartment in which he travelled.

questions.question: Who offered help to Velu when he was sitting on the Chennai station platform?
questions.explanation: A girl of his own age approached Velu as she noticed him sitting on a bench, sad and lonely, and offered to help him find food.

questions.question: Who was Jaya in the plot, Children at work?
questions.explanation: Jaya was around eleven years old and she was compelled to do rag picking to earn a living. She was poverty-stricken and was leading a miserable life.

questions.question: How was Jaya portrayed in the plot, Children at work?
questions.explanation: Jaya was a poor child who owing to her circumstances was compelled to work as a rag picker. But she was generous enough to help Velu and guide him to earn a living. She was courageous enough to face the harsh realities of the world around her with a smile.

questions.question: What was the name of the girl who met Velu at the railway station in Chennai?
questions.explanation: The girl who approached and offered to help Velu was Jaya. She was of Velu's age and was shabbily dressed.

questions.question: What was Jaya carrying when she met Velu at the Chennai station?
questions.explanation: Jaya had a huge sack on one of her shoulders when she met Velu for the first time. She was picking up dirty plastic cups from the floor and stuffing the sack.

questions.question: Why did Velu travel without a ticket to Chennai?
questions.explanation: Velu ran away from his home and managed to board a train to Chennai. He was penniless and so couldn't afford to buy the ticket.

questions.question: Where did Jaya lead Velu from the railway station in search of food?
questions.explanation: Jaya took Velu to a marriage hall, as she knew that they would be able to find something to eat there. She too was penniless like Velu and couldn't afford to buy food but she surely knew how to survive.

questions.question: What did Jaya and Velu fetch to eat from the marriage hall?
questions.explanation: Jaya managed to find two bananas and Vada from a big garbage bin overflowing with rubbish that was located behind the marriage hall.

questions.question: What materials were the strange huts made up of in the plot, Children at work?
questions.explanation: Velu was astounded to see the huts which were built of all sorts of things like- metal sheets, tyres, bricks, wood and plastic. The huts appeared strange to Velu as he had never seen such houses before.

questions.question: What did Jaya give to Velu so that he could earn a living?
questions.explanation: Jaya took Velu inside one of the huts and handed him an old pair of shoes without laces, a stick, and a sack so, that Velu could also assist her in the task of rag-picking.

questions.question: What social evil does the author try to highlight in the plot, Children at work?
questions.explanation: The author highlighted the problem of child labour existing in our society. He reflected the pathetic conditions in which children are compelled to work owing to poverty and hunger.

questions.question: The scene of the railway platform reminded Velu

options.option: that he was alone in the big crowd
that this city was so different from his village

questions.explanation: In all his eleven years, Velu had never seen so many people, except once a year at the fair in his village.

questions.question: Velu got into the train
questions.explanation: Velu got on the train without a ticket as he had no money for a ticket but luckily the ticket collector didn’t come.

questions.question: Velu had run away from home because

options.option: he could not stand his father’s beatings
there was no work in the village

questions.explanation: Velu had run away because he couldn’t stand his father beating him for one more day.

questions.question: Velu ran after Jaya because

options.option: he wanted to give Jaya to police
Jaya had asked him to do so
he had no idea where to go

questions.explanation: Velu ran after Jaya because he had no idea where to go.

questions.question: Jaya had to drag Velu to the other side because

options.option: she did not want to lose Velu

questions.explanation: Jaya had to drag Velu to the other side because he kept hesitating to cross.

questions.question: What work had Velu done before?
questions.explanation: The only work he had ever done was on the landowner’s farm, weeding and taking cows out to graze.

questions.question: What were the huts made of? (Children at Work)
questions.explanation: The huts were built out of all sorts of things — metal sheets, tyres, bricks, wood, and plastic.

questions.question: Who was fighting over a banana leaf? (Children at Work)
questions.explanation: Two goats were standing on the pile, fighting for a banana leaf.

questions.question: The girl grinned and pointed at a huge building. Which building did she point at? (Children at Work)
questions.explanation: The girl grinned and pointed at a huge building which was the Central Jail.

questions.question: What was the girl(Jaya) wearing? (Children at Work)
questions.explanation: Venu found behind him a girl around his own age, wearing a long banian that came down to her knees.

questions.question: What did Velu feel when he was on the platform?
questions.explanation: Velu felt miserable and exhausted when he was on the platform.

questions.question: What was the girl picking up from the platform?
questions.explanation: She was picking up dirty plastic cups from the floor and stuffing them into her sack.

questions.question: Why was Velu unable to read the signs?

options.option: He did not know how to read

questions.explanation: Venu was unable to read the signs because they were in English.

questions.question: What did Jaya collect?
questions.explanation: Jaya was a ragpicker and collected things like paper, plastic, glass.

questions.question: Till when did Velu decide to do the work?

options.option: Till he could find a better job
Till he could get something to eat

questions.explanation: Velu decided to do the work until he could find a better job.

subtopics.name: It So Happened 8 - The Selfish Giant

questions.question: There was a beautiful garden and children loved to play in it. Whose garden was this?
Why did the Giant build a high wall round his lovely garden?

options.option: To stop animals enter into the garden
To stop children enter into the garden
To stop parents enter into the garden
To stop spring and summer enter into the garden

questions.explanation: To stop children enter into the garden

questions.question: The giant's garden was ________. (The selfish giant)
questions.explanation: The garden was lovely. It had soft green grass and beautiful flowers all around. It lured the children and they sneaked in after school every afternoon.

questions.question: How was the giant portrayed to be initially in the story?
questions.explanation: The giant was projected to be very selfish, initially in the story. He did not allow the children to enter and play in his garden. He wanted to keep his garden reserved for himself.

questions.question: How many peach trees stood in the giant's garden?
questions.explanation: The giant's garden had twelve peach trees that bore rich fruits in the season of autumn and looked beautiful in the season of spring.

questions.question: How did the children react to hearing the songs of birds in the giant's garden?
questions.explanation: The children were mesmerized by the music of birds and ceased to play and listened to the birds' song.

questions.question: When did the children visit the giant's garden?
questions.explanation: Every afternoon, as the children returned from their school, they used to sneak inside the garden and play.

questions.question: Where did the children start playing after their entry to the giant's garden was restricted?
questions.explanation: The children tried to play on the road but it was very dusty and full of hard stones, so, they did not like to play over there and missed playing inside the garden.

questions.question: How long did the giant stay at his friend's place?
questions.explanation: The giant was away from his garden for seven years. He had visited his friend and stayed for a period of seven years with him.

questions.question: Where was the giant gone for seven years in the plot, The selfish giant?
questions.explanation: The giant had gone to visit his friend, the cornish ogre, and stayed with him for seven years.

questions.question: How did the giant restrict the children from entering his garden?

options.option: By fencing it with thorny bushes all around
By building a high wall around the garden
By constructing a big iron gate on the entrance
By posting security guards around the garden

questions.explanation: The giant prohibited the children from entering his garden, so, he built a high wall all around the garden and put up a notice-board that read, 'TRESPASSERS WILL BE PROSECUTED'.

questions.question: How did the giant react, when he saw the children playing in his garden initially?
questions.explanation: The giant was selfish, he wanted to reserve the garden only for himself. He was full of rage when he spotted the children playing in his garden and thereafter banned their entry inside his garden.

questions.question: What effect did the banning of children in the giant's garden have on the garden?
questions.explanation: The giant prohibited the entry of the children inside his garden. As a result, the garden appeared to be lifeless. There was no sign of joy and greenery.

questions.question: Which season persisted in the giant's garden, when he restricted the entry of children in his garden?
questions.explanation: The season of winter never ended in the giant's garden after the banning of children. The entire garden was covered with snow and frost. There were no traces of joy and greenery in the garden anymore.

questions.question: How did the garden appear during the prolonged winter season in the plot, The selfish giant?
questions.explanation: The giant's garden was cold and white due to the never-ending winters. It looked deserted and desolated as there were no signs of greenery and happiness inside the garden.

questions.question: The frost painted all the trees ________. (The selfish giant)
questions.explanation: The author personified the frost and projected it to cover the trees that stood in the giant's garden completely as if the frost painted the trees with silver.

questions.question: What was the source of the sweet music that the giant heard, one morning?
questions.explanation: One morning, the giant heard some lovely music that sounded very sweet to him. A little linnet was singing outside his window. The music seemed very beautiful to him as he had not heard a bird sing in his garden for a long time.

questions.question: How did the children manage to enter the garden after their prohibition inside? (The selfish giant)

options.option: Through a little hole in the wall

questions.explanation: The children crept in through a little hole in the high wall that the giant had built all around the garden to restrict their entry.

questions.question: Who managed to melt the giant's heart eventually in the plot, The selfish giant​​​​​​?
questions.explanation: The giant's heart was melted by the little boy who stood crying in his garden for he was not able to climb the tree covered with snow and frost. The little boy was the first to hug and kiss the giant.

questions.question: The children stopped their games to (The Selfish Giant)
questions.explanation: The birds sat on the trees and sang so sweetly that the children used to stop their games in order to listen to them.

questions.question: The Giant was angry when he saw the
questions.explanation: The Giant was angry when he saw the children playing.

questions.question: The Giant thought that the children in his garden were
questions.explanation: The Giant thought that the children in his garden were trespassers.

questions.question: Nature was unhappy with the giant because

options.option: he did not water the plants properly to play

questions.explanation: Nature was unhappy with the giant because he did not allow the children.

questions.question: In the selfish giant’s garden there was always
questions.explanation: In the selfish giant’s garden there was always winter.

questions.question: The sweet music which the giant heard was of
questions.explanation: The sweet music which the giant heard was of a little linnet.

questions.question: When did the people notice the giant playing with children?

options.option: While going to the market at twelve o'clock

questions.explanation: When the people were going to market at twelve o’clock they found the Giant playing with the children in the most beautiful garden they had ever seen.

questions.question: What gestures of the boy enlightened the Giant?
questions.explanation: The little boy stretched out his two arms and flung them round the Giant’s neck (hugged him), and kissed him. This enlightened the Giant.

questions.question: Why did the Autumn not give golden fruit to the Giant’s garden?
questions.explanation: The Autumn did not give golden fruit to the Giant’s garden because the Giant was too selfish.

questions.question: Which colour was the hail dressed in?
questions.explanation: The Hail was dressed in grey, and his breath was like ice.

questions.question: What did Frost do?

options.option: Covered the grass with a white cloak
Rattle on the roof of the castle

questions.explanation: The Snow covered up the grass with her great white cloak, and Frost painted all the trees silver.

questions.question: What did the Snow do?

options.option: Covered the grass with a white cloak
Rattle on the roof of the castle

questions.explanation: The Snow covered up the grass with her great white cloak, and the Frost painted all the trees silver.

questions.question: Why did the flower slip back into the ground?
questions.explanation: Once a beautiful flower put its head out from the grass, but when it saw the notice-board it was so sorry for the children that it slipped back into the ground again, and went off to sleep.

questions.question: Which season was it in the garden when it was spring all over the country?
questions.explanation: When the Spring came all over the country, it was still Winter in the garden of the Selfish Giant.

questions.question: Where else did the children try to play?
questions.explanation: The children tried to play on the road, but the road was very dusty and full of hard stones, and they did not like it.

questions.question: What was the child's garden like? (The Selfish Giant)
Who, according to the Giant, were the most beautiful flowers of all?
questions.explanation: The children were the most beautiful flowers of all according to the Giant.

questions.question: Who were the only people that were pleased?
questions.explanation: The only people who were pleased were the Snow and the Frost.

questions.question: What did the North Wind do?

options.option: Rattle on the roof of the castle
Covered the grass with a white cloak

questions.explanation: The North Wind roared all day about the garden, and blew the chimney-pots down.

questions.question: What was the North Wind wrapped in?

subtopics.name: It So Happened 8 - The Treasure Within

questions.question: Who is Mr Hafeez in The Treasure Within?
questions.explanation: Mr Hafeez Contractor is one of India’s leading architects.

questions.question: Who suggested Mr. Hafeez, to drop everything and join architecture?
questions.explanation: His cousin’s husband suggested Mr. Hafeez, to drop everything and join architecture.

questions.question: Who interviewed Mr. Hafeez Contractor in the chapter, The treasure within?
questions.explanation: Mr. Bela Raja, the editor of Sparsh, a newsletter from the resource centre interviewed Hafeez Contractor in the given chapter.

questions.question: For how many years did Hafeez Contractor study French?
questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor spent seven years learning French though he failed to use it fluently or comprehend it correctly.

questions.question: Who was Behram Divecha? (The treasure within)
questions.explanation: Behram Divecha was a friend of Hafeez Contractor. The two used to have competitions for designing forts, guns, and ammunition.

questions.question: In which year did Hafeez Contractor begin his career?
questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor commenced his career as an architect in the year 1977 under the apprenticeship of T. Khareghat.

questions.question: Which language did Hafeez Contractor initially opt at Jaihind college?
questions.explanation: Hafeez opted for German at Jaihind college. He had a choice between German and French and he did not like French, so, he thought of trying to learn German instead.

questions.question: Which subject did Hafeez Contractor detest the most during his schooling?
questions.explanation: The subject gave Hafeez nightmares. He was never good at mathematics and the subject gave him shivers. He used to get nightmares when he used to appear for a mathematics exam and he was not prepared for it.

questions.question: In which profession is Hafeez Contractor engaged?
questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor is a leading architect of India. He had an architectural acumen from a very tender age. He was able to project his thoughts on paper very well though he struggled with his studies.

questions.question: Whose words marked a life-changing impact on Hafeez Contractor?
questions.explanation: The principal made Hafeez conscious of the hardships his mother had faced to raise him after his father's death and that now it was Hafeez's turn to take care of his mother. This was a turning point in Hafeez's life.

questions.question: Which sports team had Hafeez Contractor captained during his schooling?
questions.explanation: Hafeez was a sportsman and a champion during his school days. He was the captain of his school's cricket team.

questions.question: What was Hafeez's score in his SSC examination?
questions.explanation: Hafeez concentrated entirely on his studies and finally, managed to secure a 50% mark in his SSC examination.

questions.question: What interested Hafeez Contractor the most during his school days?
questions.explanation: Hafeez was not at all interested in studies, he was only interested in playing or in trying pranks on others. In fact, he tried his level best to distract others.

questions.question: Who advised Hafeez Contractor to complete his graduation instead of joining the police force?
questions.explanation: Hafeez's mother asked him not to join the police but just do his graduation.

questions.question: Which college did Hafeez Contractor join after his schooling?
questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor got admitted to Jaihind college in Mumbai, by using someone's influence.

questions.question: Why did the students book Hafeez Contractor's books in advance?

options.option: He used to give them for free
He sold his old books at a 50% discount

questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor hardly opened his books to study, except a little before his exams. His juniors used to order his books in advance as they were technically as good as brand new.

questions.question: Who told Hafeez Contractor that he could become a good architect during his school days?
questions.explanation: His teacher, Mrs. Gupta told him that he had the potential of becoming a good architect as she noticed Hafeez's sketches.

questions.question: Which drawing was flawed according to Hafeez Contractor during his visit to the architect's office?
questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor pointed out the mistake in the drawing of a window at the architect's office when he visited there to learn French.

questions.question: Under whom did Hafeez Contractor commence his career?
questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor began his career with Mr T. Khareghat as an apprentice architect and in the year 1977, he became the associate partner in the same firm.

questions.question: Bela Raja was an editor of which newsletter when he interviewed Hafeez Contractor?
questions.explanation: Mr Bela Raja was the editor of Sparsh, a newsletter from the Resource Center, The Valley School, Bangalore.

questions.question: The contractor had nightmares about (The Treasure Within)
questions.explanation: The contractor had nightmares about appearing for a maths examination.

questions.question: The contractor’s earliest memory about school is that

options.option: he always passed with the first division
from the third standard when he lost interest

questions.explanation: The contractor’s earliest memory about school is that from the third standard when he lost interest in studies.

questions.question: The contractor loved
The contractor passed most of his school examination by (The Treasure Within)
questions.explanation: The contractor passed most of his school examination by copying.

questions.question: Contractor’s teachers
questions.explanation: The contractor’s teachers gave him a caning every week.

questions.question: The contractor would plan strategies for
questions.explanation: The contractor would plan strategies for fighting against a gang.

questions.question: What did Hafeez and his friend compete about?
questions.explanation: Hafeez had a friend called Behram Divecha. There used to have competitions between two of them for designing forts, guns and ammunition.

questions.question: Why did Hafeez not join the Army?

options.option: His mother advised him to do graduation
His aunt tore up the admission letter

questions.explanation: He wanted to join the Army and had even got his admission letter but his aunt tore it up.

questions.question: Hafeez was
questions.explanation: Hafeez used to be a very good sportsman. He had been the senior champion for so many years and was also the cricket captain.

questions.question: What is the full form of HC? (The Treasure Within)
questions.explanation: HC is used for Hafeez Contractor in the text of the interview.

questions.question: What is the full form of BR? (The Treasure Within)
questions.explanation: BR is Ms Bela Raja, editor of Sparsh, a newsletter from the Resource Centre.

questions.question: What was Mrs. Gupta’s advice to Hafeez?

options.option: To join the police force upon growing up
To join the Army upon growing up
To become an architect upon growing up

questions.explanation: Mrs Gupta, one of my teachers in school, had said Hafeez that although he was useless in everything else but his sketches were good and advised him to become an architect upon growing up.

questions.question: What does Ms. Bela think Hafeez uses while dealing with his clients?
questions.explanation: Ms. Bela thinks Hafeez identifies his clients' tastes instinctively (his natural instincts).

questions.question: Why did Hafeez Contractor not join the Police force?

options.option: His aunt tore up the admission letter
His mother advised him to do graduation

questions.explanation: Hafeez Contractor did not join the Police force because his mother advised him to do graduation.

questions.question: Who was an unhappy schoolboy? (The Treasure Within)
How did Hafeez create a distraction?
questions.explanation: Hafeez created a distraction by playing ‘chor police’.

questions.question: What did Hafeez receive every week?
Princess September loves the bird far too much to take chances, and acts upon her sisters’ advice. What did her sister advise?
questions.explanation: Her sister advised putting the bird into a cage.

questions.question: How many daughters did the king of Siam have? (Princess September)
questions.explanation: A royal couple of the kingdom of Siam was blessed with nine daughters, whom they named after the months of a year.

questions.question: Which peculiar habbit did the king of Siam follow on his birthday every year in the plot, Princess September?
questions.explanation: The king had a peculiar habbit of giving gifts on his birthday instead of receiving them.

questions.question: Which bird was gifted by the king to each of his daughters on his birthday in the plot, Princess September?
questions.explanation: The king gifted a green and yellow parrot in a golden cage to each of his daughters on his birthday.

questions.question: What happened to Princess September's parrot?
questions.explanation: One morning when Princess September went to say good morning to her parrot, she saw it lying dead at the bottom of it's golden cage.

questions.question: How did princess September react when her parrot died?
questions.explanation: Princess September was shattered and she burst into a flood of tears when she found her parrot lying dead at the bottom of its golden cage.

questions.question: What pulled princess September out of the gloom of her parrot's death?
questions.explanation: A little bird that came hopping into the princess's room managed to pull her out of the gloom. The bird sang so beautifully that princess September got mesmerized and she got rid of the agony she was suffering because of the death of her parrot.

questions.question: With whom did the king compare his councilors with, in the plot, Princess September?
questions.explanation: The king felt that his councillors sang vain praises of him. Just like the parrots, even the councillors could repeat words and phrases in seven different languages, but unfortunately, they never meant anything that they uttered.

questions.question: How did princess September's sisters react to seeing the little bird?
questions.explanation: The other princesses were jealous of princess September's little bird for the bird could sing beautifully. The king too praised the little bird which made the other princesses all the more envious of their sister and the little bird that she found.

questions.question: Who suggested princess September to put her little bird in a cage?
questions.explanation: The eight sisters of princess September grew jealous of her on seeing the little bird that she had found. They advised her to put the bird in a cage due to safety concerns.

questions.question: Where had the bird gone when princess September's sisters came to meet her?

options.option: To the green fields with his family
To pay a visit to his father-in-law

questions.explanation: The little bird went to pay a visit to his father-in-law when the other princesses came to meet princess September.

questions.question: In how many different Oriental languages could the parrots repeat the phrase, God save the king? (Princess September)
questions.explanation: The princesses taught their parrots to say the phrase, 'God saves the king' and 'pretty Polly' in seven different Oriental languages.

questions.question: Whom did the other princesses get married to in the plot, Princess September?
questions.explanation: Princess September's eight sisters always kept their room's windows shut, so, they grew up to be rather ugly and were married to the king's councillors in the plot.

questions.question: On which animal was princess September carried and taken away to the city where the king of Cambodia lived?
questions.explanation: Princess September got married to the king of Cambodia and she was carried on a white elephant and taken all the way to the city where the king resided.

questions.question: Why did princess September grow up to be very beautiful?

options.option: Because of the herbal products that she used
Because of the rich diet, she took
For she always kept her window open

questions.explanation: The princess kept her window open throughout the day for the bird to come and sing in its beautiful voice for her. This had a positive effect on her due to which her beauty was enhanced and she grew up to be extremely beautiful.

questions.question: What did princess September do to the bird after it returned after meeting its father-in-law?

options.option: The princess put the bird in a cage
The princess yelled at it for being late

questions.explanation: The princess put her little friend in a cage, after it returned from its father-in-law's place for the princess became highly insecure that the bird might leave her or some mishap could occur, this thought drove her to captivate the little bird.

questions.question: What reason did princess September provide to the bird for capturing it in a cage?

options.option: A cobra was slithering inside the palace
An eagle was spotted in the palace

questions.explanation: The princess captivated the poor little bird on the pretext of saving it from some cats prowling outside.

questions.question: What did the bird's desire for when it was put in a cage by princess September?
questions.explanation: The bird longed for independence and it detested being put inside the cage. It craved for freedom to fly and sing without any restrictions.

questions.question: What was highlighted by the story, Princess September?
questions.explanation: The author highlighted the importance of freedom in the plot. He craftily focused on the plight of the little bird that was captivated by the princess. The bird became lifeless and could not sing inside the cage in spite of all the comforts that it was provided in the golden cage. It detested the cage and craved for its freedom.

questions.question: Princess September got a green parrot in a golden cage:
questions.explanation: Princess September got a green parrot in a golden cage on the king's birthday.

questions.question: When the parrot died, the Queen:
questions.explanation: When the parrot died, the Queen punished Princess September.

questions.question: A little bird came into the room of Princess September when:

options.option: her maids of Honour opened the window

questions.explanation: A little bird came into the room of Princess September when the Princess was crying and hungry.

questions.question: When the little bird had finished his song, the Princess:
questions.explanation: When the little bird had finished his song, the Princess forgot her hunger.

questions.question: The general opinion in the King’s palace was that:

options.option: the bird sang much better than the parrots
the bird should be put in the cage

questions.explanation: The general opinion in the King’s palace was that the bird sang much better than the parrots.

questions.question: All the sisters of Princess September offered to buy her a parrot because:

options.option: they really wanted her to have a pet bird
the Queen had asked them to do so
the King had asked them to do so

questions.explanation: All the sisters of Princess September offered to buy her a parrot because they were jealous of the bird.

questions.question: Whom did Princess September marry?
questions.explanation: Princess September was married to the King of Cambodia.

questions.question: The next morning, when Princess September tried to wake him up, he looked as if he was:
questions.explanation: The next morning, when Princess September tried to wake him (the bird) up, he looked as if he was dead.

questions.question: Why did the bird reject September’s offer to take him out every day?

options.option: He did not like the view through the bars of the cage

questions.explanation: He did not like the view through the bars of the cage.

questions.question: On what condition did the bird agree to stay in all night?

options.option: If she let him out in the morning
If she kept him in it forever

questions.explanation: The bird agreed to stay in all night only if she let him out in the morning.

questions.question: Where had the little bird gone? (Princess September)
questions.explanation: The bird had gone to visit his father-in-law.

questions.question: What offer did the other Princesses make to Princess September?

options.option: To buy her a green and yellow parrot

questions.explanation: The other Princesses promise Princess September to buy her green and yellow parrot.

questions.question: What did the parrots remind the King of?
questions.explanation: According to the king the parrots reminded him too much of his Councillors​​​​​​​.

questions.question: How many hour(s) did the Princesses give to their parrots each day?
questions.explanation: The princesses were very proud of their parrots and they spent an hour every day teaching the parrots to talk.

questions.question: How did the Princess feel when the little bird asked to be her pet?

questions.explanation: Ranji discovered the pool when it was the peak of summer and the weather was extremely hot.

questions.question: In which town was the pool discovered by Ranji?
questions.explanation: Ranji discovered the pool in the forests of Rajpur, a semi-hill station. Ranji while wandering about the hills and forests that stretched away endlessly on all sides of the town spotted the pool, one fine day.

questions.question: What was Ranji wearing on the day he discovered the pool?
questions.explanation: The day Ranji discovered the pool, the weather was extremely hot, so, he has dressed accordingly in a vest and shorts to feel cool and comfortable.

questions.question: Where did Ranji reside before he came to stay at Rajpur?
questions.explanation: Ranji had lived with his parents in an arid town in the middle of the Rajputana desert.

questions.question: How did the pool appear to Ranji when he discovered it?
questions.explanation: The pool instantly captured Ranji's attention for it was sparkling clean and was filled with cold water that was provided by a perennial source from the hills. He could not resist and get into the pool without any hesitation.

questions.question: What did Ranji notice at the bottom of the pool?
questions.explanation: The water in the pool had a gentle translucency and Ranji could easily spot the smooth round pebbles that were lying at the bottom of the pool.

questions.question: Whom did Ranji meet at the pool?
questions.explanation: Ranji noticed an unknown boy standing a little distance away, staring at him when he visited the pool the very next day after he discovered it in the forest.

questions.question: How was the boy's behaviour towards Ranji when he noticed Ranji for the first time besides the pool?
questions.explanation: The boy stared at Ranji in a very hostile manner when he noticed him beside the pool. He spoke in a rude and aggressive manner with Ranji and was reluctant to share the pool with him.

questions.question: How were the pools in Rajputana village? (The Fight)
questions.explanation: The pools in Rajputana village were sticky, muddy pools, where buffaloes wallowed and women washed clothes.

questions.question: How was Ranji projected to be physically in the plot, The fight?
questions.explanation: Ranji's limbs were supple, free from fat but he was strong. He could dive into the water swiftly with perfection and his dark body glistened in patches of sunlit water.

questions.question: How was Suraj described in the plot, The fight?
questions.explanation: Suraj was tall and well built. He had a broad nose and his lips were thick and red in colour. He was strong and possessed a thick set of well-built muscles.

questions.question: What did Ranji have in the bazaar?
questions.explanation: Ranji slipped into the market in the evening after he had a brawl with Suraj at the pool. He relished a bottle of lemonade and some hot, sweet jalebis in the bazaar.

questions.question: What was Ranji's first impulse when he saw his adversary in the market?
questions.explanation: Ranji noticed Suraj in the market and his first impulse was to turn away and look elsewhere. He simply wanted to ignore him initially after the ugly episode at the pool.

questions.question: What did Ranji intend to throw at Suraj, when he spotted him in the market?
questions.explanation: Ranji had just finished the lemonade when he spotted his adversary coming down the street in the market. He wished to throw that lemonade bottle in a fit of rage at his foe but somehow managed to control.

questions.question: How did Ranji and Suraj react to seeing each other at the market?

options.option: They turned away and chose not to look at each other

questions.explanation: Ranji saw Suraj at the market in the evening on the very same day when they had an ugly fight at the pool, in the midst of the forest. Both of them controlled their rage and simply scowled back at each other with equal ferocity in the bazaar.

questions.question: How many rupees did the boy request from God in the story Jalebis? Did God help the boy?
questions.explanation: No, he got no help from God.

questions.question: In which standard did the author study at that time of the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The writer was in the fifth standard at the government school in Kambelpur when the episode happened.

questions.question: Where did the writer study in the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The writer went to a government school in Kambelpur, now called Atak when the Jalebis incident occurred.

questions.question: How much money did the boy carry to his school on the day of Jalebis's episode?
questions.explanation: The boy carried four rupees on that particular day in his pocket to pay his school fees.

questions.question: Why did the boy carry four rupees to his school in the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The boy carried four rupees in his pocket to pay his school fees and funds however, he ended up spending the money to purchase Jalebis.

questions.question: Who was the incharge of collecting the school fees in the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: Master Ghulam Mohammed was the teacher who was assigned the task of collecting school fees in the writer's school when the Jalebis episode occurred.

questions.question: Who was Ghulam Mohammed in the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: Master Ghulam Mohammed was a teacher in the government school where the writer studied in the plot. He was a disciplinarian and quite strict as a teacher.

questions.question: Who could have caught the boy as he was buying the jalebis?
questions.explanation: The boy saw his paternal uncle, his father's younger brother, or his Chacha Jaan returning from the court on his tonga when he was purchasing the jalebis. In case his Chacha Jaan had seen him, he would have been caught red-handed.

questions.question: To whom did the boy compare himself to while distributing the jalebis to the children?
questions.explanation: While distributing the jalebis, the boy compared himself to Governor Saheb, who used to distribute rice to the poor on Independence Day.

questions.question: Why didn't the boy pay his fees on the day he brought money to school in the plot, Jalebis?

options.option: The teacher who was supposed to collect the fees was on a leave

He dropped the money on the route by mistake

questions.explanation: The boy failed to pay his school fees on the day he brought the money because the teacher who was supposed to collect the fees money was on leave that particular day.

questions.question: What did the boy do with the remaining jalebis, after his stomach was full?
questions.explanation: The boy couldn't eat all the jalebis as they were too many and his stomach was overfilled, so, he distributed the remaining jalebis to the boys who had assembled in the market lane.

questions.question: How was the schoolboy portrayed in the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The schoolboy was a meritorious student and received a scholarship from his school. He was honest and God-fearing for he tried his best to resist the temptation to do wrong but unfortunately gave in.

questions.question: How were the jalebis described in the story, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The writer described the Jalebis as hot, fresh, and syrupy. They were extraordinary in taste and the writer, when he was a schoolboy failed to resist them and spent his school fees on purchasing them.

questions.question: How did the boy respond to the coins initially, when their clattering tried to convince him to buy the Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The boy was tempted by the delicious jalebis and the coins clattering in his pocket tried to lure him to buy the jalebis. The boy tried his level best to ignore the coins and moved towards his home.

questions.question: Which religion was followed by the schoolboy in the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The boy belonged to a respected Muslim family in the plot. He was God-fearing and was seen praying and begging Allah to help him and save him from all the trouble that he had landed into due to his innocent desires.

questions.question: What message is given by the story, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The protagonist got lured by the mouthwatering jalebis and that is how he landed into big trouble. The moral of the story is that we should think carefully before doing an action and not to lie.

questions.question: What happened to the scholarship that the boy was supposed to get in the plot, Jalebis?
questions.explanation: The boy had planned to pay his school fees with the scholarship amount that he was supposed to receive in that month. Unfortunately, the scholarship money got postponed by a month and the boy was in trouble.

questions.question: Whom did the schoolboy plead for help to pay his school fees after his scholarship got postponed? (Jalebis)
questions.explanation: The boy pleaded to God or Allah Miyan as he addressed him, to help him out of that grave situation by ordering a Farishta or angel to pass by and drop just four rupees in his pocket, which he promised would be used only for the payment of his school fees.

questions.question: What made the child control his greed for jalebis at first?
questions.explanation: The child controls his greed for jalebis at first by thinking about his and his family’s reputation.

questions.explanation: The oldest coin said, "Sweets with the fees money, fees with the scholarship money. End of story! Kissa khatam, paisa hazam.”

questions.explanation: Master Ghulam Mohammed announced that the fees would be taken during the recess.

questions.explanation: The oldest coin asked the author to buy jalebis with the fees money and pay fees with the scholarship money.

subtopics.name: It So Happened 8 - Ancient Education System of India

questions.question: Which of the following was not a known University in ancient India?
questions.explanation: Bhuvaneshwar was not a university in ancient India.

questions.question: What was Panini's profession?
questions.explanation: Panini was the legendary Indian grammarian who was an expert in language and grammar.

questions.question: Which book was authored by Panini?
questions.explanation: Panini was a legendary Indian grammarian during the ancient times of India. He was an expert in language and grammar and authored one of the greatest works on grammar called Ashtadhyayi.

questions.question: What was Jivaka's profession?
questions.explanation: Jivaka was one of the most renowned physicians in ancient India.

questions.question: Chanakya was also known as ________.
questions.explanation: Chanakya, a skilled exponent of statecraft was also known as kautilya.

questions.question: Takshashila was also known as ________.
questions.explanation: Takshashila was a noted centre of learning in ancient India. It was also known as Taxila.

questions.question: When was Takshashila destroyed?
questions.explanation: Takshashila continued to attract students from around the world until its destruction in the 5th century CE.

questions.question: Who discovered the ruins of Takshashila?
questions.explanation: Archaeologist Alexander Cunningham discovered the ruins of Takshashila in the mid-19th century.

questions.question: What was Nalanda University known as when Xuan Zang visited it?
questions.explanation: The university was called Nala when Chinese scholar, Xuan Zang visited it.

questions.question: Who were I-Qing and Xuan Zang?
questions.explanation: The two Chinese scholars visited Nalanda University in the 7th century CE. They gave vivid accounts of Nalanda.

questions.question: Which subject did Xuan Zang study at Nalanda university?
questions.explanation: Xuan Zang, a Chinese scholar visited Nalanda and became a student of the university to study Yogashastra.​​

questions.question: Who was claimed to be the highest living authority in yoga by Xuan Zang?
questions.explanation: Xuan Zang mentioned that the chancellor of Nalanda, Shilabhadra was the highest living authority in yoga then.

questions.question: What fee was charged for education in ancient India?
questions.explanation: Knowledge was considered to be sacred in ancient India and no fee was charged for imparting it.

questions.question: Where did nuns and monks receive their education in ancient India?
questions.explanation: Many monasteries or Viharas were set up for monks and nuns to meditate, debate and discuss with the learned to quench their thirst for knowledge during the ancient times.

questions.question: What served as a center of learning and teaching in the south of India during ancient times?
questions.explanation: Agrahara was a big institution, a whole settlement of learned brahmins, with its own powers of government and was maintained by generous donations from the society.

questions.question: Who found India to be a land of wonder?
questions.explanation: Travellers from different regions were fascinated by India right from early times. The fame of Indian culture, wealth, art, and architecture, as well as its educational practices, had spread far and wide.

questions.question: What were the sources of education in ancient India?
questions.explanation: The ancient system of education in India was delivered by the Vedas, Brahmanas, Upanishads and Dharmasutras.

questions.question: When laura said something about her parent’s surprise sixtieth anniversary party, she let the cat out of the bag. The meaning of the underlined word:

options.option: Laura didn’t tell anyone about the party
Laura’s cat was hiding inside a bag.

questions.explanation:

When Laura said something about his parents' surprise sixtieth anniversary party, she revealed a secret.

questions.question: I am going to brush up on my computer skills before the job interview. The meaning of the underlined word:
questions.explanation:

questions.question: The meaning of the idiom "A poor workman blames his tools" is:

options.option: A person who blames others for his failures and A person who cannot use the tools properly.
A person who blames others for his failures.
A person who cannot use the tools properly.
A person who’s tool are of no use.

questions.explanation: "A poor workman always blames his tools" is a proverb that suggests a poor workman tends to look for an excuse for his poor work. So he always blames others for his faliures.

questions.question: The meaning of the phrase "Action speaks louder than words" is:

options.option: Action is a better speaker than word.
One should implement his actions, rather than keep talking about them.

questions.explanation:
The phrase "action speaks louder than words" means 'it is better to do something rather than just talk'. We can also use this phrase in a sentence-
It's no use sitting and speaking of success, you have to act on it. Remember actions speak louder than words.

questions.question: The meaning of the phrase "A penny saved is a penny earned" is:

options.option: One must understand the importance of saving money.
A saved penny earns you the other one.
A saved penny earns you the other one and one must understand the importance of saving money.

questions.explanation:
The phrase "a penny saved is a penny earned." means 'it is wise to save money'. We can use this phrase in a sentence:
Now that he has financial difficulties he will realize that a penny saved is a penny earned.

questions.question: The meaning of the phrase "A person is known by the company he keeps" is:

options.option: A person is known by the company he works for.
One is judged according to the friends he has.

questions.explanation:
The phrase "a man is known by the company he keeps" means 'our character is reflected in our choice of friends'. We can use this phrase in a sentence also:
If you want to know what kind of person George is, look at his friends. A person is known by the company he keeps.

questions.question: One who does nothing more than build castles in the air cannot achieve much. Meaning of the underlined proverb:

options.option: To buy  a castle on high rise

questions.explanation: Castles in the air means plans that have very little chance of happening

questions.question: The meaning of the phrase "A picture paints a thousand words" is:

options.option: One thousand words are painted in the picture.
A word presentation is far more descriptive than picture.
The picture is well versed in painting.
A visual presentation is far more descriptive than words.

questions.explanation:
The phrase "a picture paints a thousand words" means 'a picture conveys information more effectively than words'. We can use this phrase in a sentence also:
The newspaper report carried more pictures of the event than text, since a picture paints a thousand words.

questions.question: The meaning of the phrase "At the drop of a hat" is:

options.option: Wiling to do something immediately and Something beneath the hat.

questions.explanation:
The phrase "at the drop of a hat" means 'something is happening instantly, without any delay'. We can use this phrase in a sentence also:
We're all packed and ready to go; we can leave at the drop of a hat.

questions.question: The meaning of the proverb "A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush" is:

options.option: A bird in the hand becomes two.
A bird in the hand will call the other two birds.

questions.explanation:
The proverb "a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush" means 'what you have is more valuable than the prospect to have something greater'. We can use this proverb in a sentence also:
You may not like your job, but don't quit merely in the hope of finding a better one. A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.

questions.question: The meaning of the idiom "Beat a dead horse" is

options.option: To force an issue that has already ended.
To force an issue that has already ended and Beat the horse which is dead.

questions.explanation:
The idiom "beat a dead horse" means 'waste energy on something that has no chance of succeeding'. We can use this idiom in a sentence also:
He keeps trying to get his manuscripts published, but I think he is beating a dead horse.

questions.question: The meaning of the idiom "Finger lickin' good" is:

options.option: Fingers are looking good and a Licking finger is a good habit.

questions.explanation:
The idiom "finger-lickin' good" means 'very tasty meal or food that tastes really really good'. We can use this idiom in a sentence also:
My sister's cooking is finger-lickin good.

questions.question: The meaning of the idiom In the heat of the moment is:

options.option: Overwhelmed by what is happening at the moment
Excited about the things to happen in the future.

questions.explanation:
The idiom "in the heat of the moment" means 'being overwhelmed by what is happening at the moment'. We can use this idiom in a sentence also:

questions.question: The speaker gave a bird’s eye view of the political conditions in the country. The meaning of the underlined idiom:
questions.explanation:

questions.explanation:

questions.question: My plan to have a new car fell through because of the price rise. Meaning of the underlined idiom:
questions.explanation:

questions.question: He has rejoined office after a week and looks rundown. Meaning of the underlined phrase:
questions.explanation:

questions.question: In summer, woollen goods are a drug in the market. Meaning of the underlined idiom:
questions.explanation:

questions.explanation: An adverb is a word that modifies an adjective, verb, or another adverb expressing manner, place, time, or degree. Generally, adverbs of degree can describe adjectives or other adverbs. Here 'really' is an adverb that describes or modifies the adjective 'intelligent'.

questions.question: I have to go to school tomorrow so I have to get up early.
questions.explanation: An adverb is a word that modifies the meaning of an adjective, verb, or other adverb expressing manner, place, time, or degree. Here 'early' is the adverb of time that describes or qualifies the verb get up.

questions.question: There was a table in the room. There were no chairs in the room. (conjunction)
